topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The office felt cavernous, the silence amplified by the hum of the fluorescent lights. I stood by the mahogany desk, the heavy leather of my portfolio suddenly feeling alien beneath my fingertips. A note, stark white against the dark wood, lay centered on the blotter. "Effective immediately, my position is concluded." My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air that did nothing to steady the tremor starting in my hands. I reread the words, searching for a hidden clause, a misinterpretation, anything that suggested a mistake. But there was only finality. I looked towards the doorway, expecting a familiar silhouette, a nod, anything that signaled a continuation of our working relationship. The hallway remained empty. My gaze dropped to the floor, my boots suddenly feeling too heavy, rooted to the spot as a cold dread began to pool in my stomach.

[story 2]

It was the sheer absence that struck me first. Professor Alistair, the man who had shaped my entire trajectory, was simply not there. His usual booming presence, the way he’d lean over my shoulder to point out a subtle flaw in my logic, was gone. I walked to his cluttered study, the scent of old paper and pipe tobacco suddenly overpowering. Everything was precisely as it had been, yet it was fundamentally altered. His favorite armchair remained untouched, the stack of annotated texts perfectly aligned, but the space he usually occupied felt hollowed out. I picked up a pen, my fingers clumsy and unresponsive, and stared at the closed door. A tightness constricted my chest, making each breath a conscious, shallow effort. I wanted to call out, to ask if everything was alright, but the sound caught in my throat, lodging itself there like a piece of grit.

[story 3]

I found the announcement tucked inside a seldom-used file cabinet. It was brief, impersonal, delivered with the same detached efficiency Alistair always employed in his correspondence. No farewell, no explanation, just a declaration of departure. My mind raced, cycling through possibilities—a sudden emergency, a professional shift, a misunderstanding I had somehow missed. I paced the length of my small apartment, the floorboards groaning under my anxious tread. My jaw was tight, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the windowsill. The quiet of the evening pressed in, heavy and suffocating. I kept glancing toward the window, half-expecting to see him materialize, to offer some context for this abrupt void in my life, but there was only the indifferent sprawl of the city lights.
